nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes with sand or fine rubble substrate adjacent to coral structure; typically observed hovering near the entrance of a shared pistol-shrimp burrow at depths of around 15-40 m.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5-8 cm deep
loose coral rubble for burrow construction
open sandy footprint near the front glass for visibility

diet.notes: Carnivorous; generally accepts a variety of meaty foods including frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped table shrimp, and small carnivore pellets or flakes. Should be fed at least twice daily in small portions. Has been reported to take time to acclimate to prepared foods after import; live or frozen options usually trigger feeding response first.

goodWith: small peaceful reef fish (firefish, cardinalfish, royal grammas)
avoidWith: large aggressive predators (groupers, lionfish, large angelfish)
boisterous bottom-dwellers that disturb the burrow
other shrimp gobies competing for the same substrate territory
triggerfish and other crustacean-hunting species (will eat the partner shrimp)

summary: A small, striking shrimp goby with red bars on a white body and a tall sail-like dorsal fin. Forms a well-known symbiotic burrow partnership with Randall's pistol shrimp on sandy reef beds.
careNotes:
The Yasha Goby is among the most popular symbiotic shrimp gobies in the hobby thanks to its modest size and vivid red-on-white coloration. It naturally pairs with a pistol shrimp — most often the Candy/Randall's Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus randalli) — which excavates and maintains a burrow while the goby stands sentry. Tiger Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus bellulus) have also been observed pairing with this goby in captivity.

To support this behaviour, an aquascape with a fine sand bed of at least 5-8 cm and a few flat pieces of rubble for the shrimp to build under is generally advised. Open sandy floor near the front of the tank lets the pair be observed.

Cross-source temperature spread: LiveAquaria states 22-26°C while FishBase reports a preferred wild range closer to 25-29°C; the recorded 23-27°C range averages these. Tank-size guidance also varies (LiveAquaria 38 L / 10 gal, BlueZoo 91 L / 24 gal); the listed 75 L minimum biases toward the larger figure because the goby benefits from a deeper sand footprint and a stable territory shared with its partner shrimp. Care level is rated 'Easy' by LiveAquaria but 'Intermediate' by BlueZoo, primarily because newly imported specimens have been reported to be slow to accept prepared foods.

A tightly-fitting lid is important — like most gobies and dartfish, S. yasha has been known to jump when startled. The species is generally considered fully reef-safe and ignores corals and ornamental invertebrates, though sand-sifting behaviour can occasionally bury low-placed frags.

Usually kept singly or as a heterosexual pair; mature males reportedly display a black spot on the pelvic fins. Avoid housing with crustacean-eating predators that would target the pistol-shrimp partner.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity but is generally considered difficult. Pairs deposit eggs inside the shared burrow and the male tends the clutch. Raising the pelagic larvae requires very small live foods (rotifers, then copepod nauplii) and is the main hurdle for hobbyist breeding success.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species page navigated from the gobies collection root provided in the manifest. Primary source for hobby water parameters, tank size minimum, temperament, and diet recommendations.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, temperament, reef safety, and care level. BlueZoo lists a larger minimum tank (24 gal vs LiveAquaria's 10 gal) and rates care as intermediate vs LiveAquaria's easy; both differences resolved by averaging toward the more cautious figure.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Gobiidae, order Gobiiformes), wild depth/habitat (15-40 m outer reef slope), maximum standard length (4.7 cm SL), and wild temperature range (24.7-28.9°C). Wild temperature ceiling drove the upper bound of 27°C in the averaged hobby range.

additional.name: Biomes In A Box
additional.notes: Third hobby source confirming water parameters, reef safety, peaceful temperament, sand-bed requirement, and lid recommendation. Notes that males display a black spot on the pelvic fin.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity but rearing pelagic larvae is the main challenge; live rotifers and copepod nauplii are generally required.
